summary: The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on Zoning Case 309, a PUD/TDP renewal for Lakeville Place Development LLC, proposing 207 single-family homes and 48 multi-family (land condominium) units on approximately 111 acres near Deerfield Road and State Route 132. The original zone change was approved in 2019 but the preliminary development plan expired due to COVID-related delays. Multiple residents voiced opposition citing traffic congestion, water runoff/flooding, infrastructure capacity, property tax concerns, and changes to community character. The commission voted to approve the preliminary development plan with the condition that all seven staff recommendations be met, forwarding the case to the Board of Trustees for their May 25, 2021 meeting.
decisions: Approved minutes from the March 23rd meeting
Approved the preliminary development plan for Zoning Case 309 (Lakeville Place Development LLC) with seven staff recommendations as conditions, forwarding to the Board of Trustees
action_items: Applicant (Scott Huber/Abercrombie & Associates and Joe Perugio) to review and comply with all seven staff recommendations regarding missing plan details
Applicant to submit landscape plans, cabana/pool details, athletic field plans, and architectural standards documentation
Applicant to complete and submit updated traffic study before the Board of Trustees meeting
Applicant to work with staff to address code compliance items in Articles 5 and 6 of the zoning resolution
Harry Holbert (staff) to forward zoning commission recommendation and agency comments to Board of Trustees for May 25, 2021 meeting
Applicant to obtain comment from Clermont County Permit Central (building and planning) which was not yet received
Applicant to address wetland delineation and Army Corps of Engineers requirements
key_amounts: 255 total units (207 single-family homes + 48 multi-family/land condominium units)
2.31 units per acre proposed density (maximum allowable is 6 units per acre under Table 6-1)
Lot sizes ranging from 8,199 sq ft to 23,340 sq ft with average of 11,395 sq ft
Homes expected to start in the high $200,000s to $350,000 range
50-foot wooded buffer surrounding the entire property
60 lots in phase one, 56 lots in phase two
28-foot street width (back to back, standard county width)
Original 2005 proposal was 330 units, reduced to 255 units
notable_topics: PUD preliminary development plan renewal for Lakeville Place Development (Zoning Case 309)
Traffic congestion concerns on Deerfield Road, State Route 132, and surrounding roads
Stormwater runoff and flooding concerns from neighboring property owners
Water pressure concerns in Heritage Woods subdivision
Infrastructure capacity (sewer, water, roads) for supporting new development
Property tax increase concerns from existing residents
Community character preservation vs. development growth
Reference to 2005 referendum where 69% of community voted against similar project
Outside agency reviews from fire chief, soil and water, health district, ODOT, Army Corps of Engineers
Wetland protection and EPA stormwater requirements
HOA-managed amenities including pool, cabana, ball fields, soccer fields, and walking trails
Lift station needed for sanitary sewer connection to Deerfield Road
Architectural review standards for home construction quality
attendees: Mr. Settle (Zoning Commission member - Terry Settle)
Scott Huber (site civil engineer, Abercrombie & Associates)
Joe Perugio (developer, Beaver Creek Site Management)
Jeff Jarvis (adjacent property owner, Brushy Fork Road)
Chief Pegram (Goshen Township Fire and EMS Chief - referenced)
John McManus (Clermont County Soil and Water Conservation District - referenced)
Robert Wiley (Clermont County Public Health - referenced)
Heath Wilson (Clermont County Water Resources - referenced)
Adam W. Buckley (US Army Corps of Engineers - referenced)
notes: This is a Goshen Township Zoning Commission hearing, not a Board of Trustees meeting. The video title references 'ZC 5421 132 Hearing' but the case discussed is Zoning Case 309. The development was originally proposed around 2005 with 330 units, went to referendum and was rejected by 69% of voters. It was re-proposed in 2019 with 255 units and the zone change was approved, but the preliminary development plan expired due to COVID delays, necessitating this renewal hearing. Strong public opposition was expressed by nearly all speakers, though one resident (Dave Bachman) spoke in favor. The commission approved forwarding the plan to the Board of Trustees with conditions despite significant public opposition. One resident (Jeff Jarvis) noted the township website was difficult to navigate to find relevant documents.
title: ZC 5421 132 Hearing
